claim: E-0113 adds a bounded project-passive coverage consumer for the existing FNT, GUI, and IE prefix/envelope descriptors. omega_tool frontend-envelope-coverage-verify-tree recursively discovers regular top-level HOG files below one supplied root using identity-guarded traversal and rejects links, Windows reparse points, unsafe entries, and identity changes. On successful discovery, every admitted top-level HOG has been read completely and represented by SHA-256 digests of its 64 KiB chunks, including a shorter final chunk. The retained 32-byte digest payload is globally limited to 262,144 entries, exactly 8 MiB. Parsing uses the reopened stable handle and one verified 64 KiB cache; bytes become parser-visible only after matching their discovery digest. Each top-level HOG receives an initial parser raw-read allowance equal to its discovered size, and each nested HOG that passes existing count, size, and aggregate limits adds the complete physical-chunk coverage of its absolute span. Existing ceilings bound parser authenticated raw reads to at most 36 GiB minus 64 KiB and total discovery-plus-parser HOG bytes requested through stable handles to at most 52 GiB minus 64 KiB. These are requested-byte rather than physical-device-I/O bounds. The unkeyed digests are consistency pins for bytes observed during discovery, not source or authorship authentication, a signature or provenance claim, or an atomic filesystem snapshot, and they do not prove the file never changed outside an authenticated read. Nested members classified by an ASCII-case-insensitive .hog suffix are followed through the established bounded HOG span parser to depth 32. Each descriptor retains its 1 MiB input ceiling; after the ceiling check the scanner reads one complete candidate into a temporary owned buffer and releases it before the next candidate. HOG indexes use bounded random access, and the scanner does not materialize a separate full-archive buffer; it owns the bounded indexes for the active nesting chain, the digest table, one verified chunk cache, and at most one candidate buffer at a time.
